basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: al-Qiyāma — “the Resurrection,” but also, inwardly, the raising up, the standing of the whole being before its Lord. The sūra opens with a twofold oath in which God swears by the Day of Resurrection and by the soul that blames itself (al-nafs al-lawwāma): that conscience which reproaches the servant his failings and acknowledges the deficiency of his state. Qushayrī opens with a luminous distinction concerning the divine name: whoever hears it through the witness of knowledge (ʿilm) gains insight; whoever hears it through the witness of gnosis (maʿrifa) is left in bewilderment — “the scholars in the stillness of His proof, the gnostics in the dazzlement of His sovereignty.” The sūra then unfolds man's doubt about his own resurrection, the harrowing scene of the death-agony — “when the breath rises to the throat” — and the parting of faces: those that, radiant, turn their gaze toward their Lord (the great verse of the vision of God), and those that are darkened. Qushayrī adds an unforgettable pearl: while men wash and bury the body of the deceased, the Real clothes his soul in the adornments it deserves, washes it with the water of mercy, and prays over it with His angels.

versets.trad: No!... I swear by the Day of Resurrection!
versets.commentaire: lā uqsimu (لا أُقْسِمُ): an emphatic oath-formula. Qushayrī: “it means: I swear by the Day of Resurrection.” The opening lā is not a true negation but a threshold: it sweeps aside what precedes — false certainties — to open onto the one certainty that counts. God swears by the Day of raising up: the standing of the whole being before Him.

mots.def: uqsimu: I swear; the lā reinforces the oath.
mots.gram: imperfect verb, 1st pers., preceded by the particle lā
noun preceded by the oath-preposition bi-

mots.def: qiyāma: raising up, resurrection; from qāma, to stand.

versets.trad: No!... I swear by the self-reproaching soul!
versets.commentaire: al-nafs al-lawwāma (النَّفْس اللَّوَّامَة): the soul that blames itself. Qushayrī defines it: “the soul that reproaches its possessor for his faults and acknowledges the deficiency of its state.” Tomorrow, he adds, every soul will blame itself — for its ingratitude or for its shortcoming. God thus swears by this inner voice of remorse: a sign that the conscience that accuses itself is already a beginning of salvation, whereas the soul satisfied with itself still sleeps.

mots.def: lāma: to blame; lawwāma: intensive form, blaming much.

versets.trad: Does man think We shall not gather his bones?
versets.commentaire: “Does man imagine We shall not raise him after his death?” glosses Qushayrī. It all starts with this supposition (yaḥsabu): man takes the limit of his imagination for the limit of the possible. Because he himself cannot remake, he believes God cannot. The doubt here is not reason but the laziness of a mind closed to what lies beyond the visible.

mots.gram: an + lan (negation of the future) + verb

versets.ar: بَلَىٰ قَادِرِينَ عَلَىٰ أَن نُّسَوِّيَ بَنَانَهُ
versets.tr: balā qādirīna ʿalā an nusawwiya banānahu
versets.trad: Yes indeed! We have the power to restore his very fingertips.
versets.commentaire: balā (بَلَى): “but yes!” — the rebuttal of doubt. banān (بَنان): the tips of the fingers. Qushayrī underlines the wonder of the detail: not only will God gather the bones, He will refashion the very fingerprints. “He who can remould the pulp of the fingers — each ridge unique — how could He not remake the whole man?” The miracle hides in the finest thing, where no one thinks to look.

mots.def: balā: affirmative particle answering a negation.
qadara: to be able, have power.
banān: the tips of the fingers.

versets.trad: And yet, man prefers to deny what lies before him.
versets.commentaire: li-yafjura amāmahu (لِيَفْجُرَ أَمامَه): lit. “to transgress before him,” that is, in the time to come. Qushayrī gives a sharp spiritual reading: man “puts the fault forward and the repentance back.” He tells himself: “I shall repent later” — then dies without having repented. As long as the taste of the fault and the plan to return to it remain in the depth of the heart, repentance is not valid: for its very knot is to renounce the return.

mots.def: fajara: to sin, debauch, break the dam.
amām: ahead, the time to come.

versets.trad: He asks: “When will the Day of Resurrection come?”
versets.commentaire: “When then, the Day of Resurrection?” Qushayrī: the question is asked “out of incredulity” (istibʿād), not out of thirst for knowledge. To ask the date is yet another way of deferring: as long as the appointed time seems far off, one thinks oneself excused from preparing. Curiosity about the when is the mask of refusal.

versets.trad: When the sight is dazzled,
versets.commentaire: bariqa l-baṣar (بَرِقَ البَصَر): the gaze is dazzled, struck with stupor. Qushayrī notes the two readings: bariqa (i), it is seized with vertigo and confusion; baraqa (a), it freezes without blinking, transfixed with horror. It is the hour when the eye, until then sovereign in the world of the visible, no longer knows where to rest: the light of the Day overwhelms it.

mots.def: bariqa: to be dazzled, struck with stupor.

versets.trad: when the moon is eclipsed,
versets.commentaire: khasafa l-qamar (خَسَفَ القَمَر): the moon is eclipsed, loses its brightness. The luminaries that ordered the night and the month are effaced. Inwardly: the borrowed glimmers — the ones the soul made do with — go out, that the one Light which is not eclipsed may remain.

mots.def: khasafa: to be eclipsed, lose its light.

versets.trad: when the sun and the moon are brought together.
versets.commentaire: jumiʿa l-shamsu wa-l-qamar (جُمِعَ الشَّمْسُ وَالقَمَر): sun and moon are joined. Qushayrī reports the striking image: they will be joined “like two slaughtered bulls,” or united in the common loss of their light. What was separated to mark out time — day and night, the star of day and that of night — is merged: time itself is abolished in the eternity that opens.

versets.trad: That Day, man will say: “Where can I flee?”
versets.commentaire: ayna l-mafarr (أَيْنَ المَفَرّ): “where is the escape?” Qushayrī evokes the moment when one is dragged toward Gehenna, when “no angel, no messenger says any longer: me, me! but: my soul, my soul!” Man casts about for a way out in every direction — but there is no longer any fleeing from the One whom one can flee only into Him.

mots.sens: the place of flight, the escape
mots.def: farra: to flee; mafarr: place to which one flees.

versets.trad: No indeed! There is no refuge!
versets.commentaire: lā wazara (لا وَزَر): no refuge. The wazar, it is explained, was the fortress or mountain where one sheltered from danger. Qushayrī: “that Day, no shelter, no flight from the decree of God.” All the ramparts man had built himself — wealth, alliances, cunning — prove vain. Nothing remains but to turn toward the One he was fleeing.

mots.def: kallā: particle of denial and warning.
wazar: shelter, fortress, mountain of refuge.

versets.trad: That Day, the resting place will be with your Lord;
versets.commentaire: al-mustaqarr (المُستَقَرّ): the place of settling, the term. Qushayrī: “no way to escape His judgement.” Yet there is a hidden sweetness here: the only place where one finally comes to rest is with your Lord. Flight has no outlet, but return does — and it is the same movement, whether one endures it or embraces it.

versets.ar: يُنَبَّأُ الْإِنسَانُ يَوْمَئِذٍ بِمَا قَدَّمَ وَأَخَّرَ
versets.tr: yunabbaʾu l-insānu yawmaʾidhin bimā qaddama wa-akhkhara
versets.trad: that Day, man will be informed of all he sent ahead and left behind.
versets.commentaire: “He will know what he sent ahead — faults God has counted, even if the servant forgot them,” glosses Qushayrī. Mā qaddama wa-akhkhara: what he advanced (deeds committed) and what he deferred (what he neglected, or the consequences left behind him). Nothing is lost from the divine memory. Our forgetting does not erase the act; it only buries it until the Day it rises again.

versets.trad: Man will be clear-sighted against himself,
versets.commentaire: A capital verse. baṣīra (بَصِيرَة): clear-sightedness, lucid witness. Qushayrī: “man is, against himself, a proof and a witness; his own limbs testify against him for what he has done.” On the Day of unveiling, no outer accuser is needed: the conscience becomes its own judge, endowed with a sight no veil can trouble. One sees oneself at last as one is — and that pitiless gaze is already the judgement.

mots.sens: a clear sight, a lucid witness

versets.trad: even though he offers his excuses.
versets.commentaire: maʿādhīr (مَعاذير): excuses, justifications. Qushayrī: even if he “brought every argument, it would not be accepted and would avail him nothing.” Before the baṣīra he has become against himself, excuses ring hollow: one no longer lies to oneself. Such is the nature of unveiling — not that words are forbidden, but that they no longer deceive anyone, beginning with oneself.

versets.ar: لَا تُحَرِّكْ بِهِ لِسَانَكَ لِتَعْجَلَ بِهِ
versets.tr: lā tuḥarrik bihi lisānaka li-taʿjala bihi
versets.trad: Do not move your tongue with it to hasten the recitation,
versets.commentaire: An aside addressed to the Prophet at the heart of the discourse on the Resurrection. Qushayrī: “Do not hasten to seize the Quran from the lips of Gabriel — for it is upon Us to gather it in your heart and fix it there.” The Prophet, fearing to forget, would race ahead of the recitation; he is taught the patience of reception. A lesson for every servant: not to outrun grace, but to receive it at its own pace.

versets.trad: it is for Us to gather it and to recite it.
themes: the Quran entrusted to the heart
versets.commentaire: jamʿahu wa-qurʾānahu (جَمْعَهُ وَقُرْآنَهُ): its gathering and its recitation. Qushayrī: “it is upon Us to gather it in your heart and preserve it there, and upon Us to make its recitation easy upon your tongue.” What is entrusted by God, God keeps. The servant need not exhaust himself in retaining: it is enough to hold out the heart; the deposit is protected by the One who lays it there.

versets.trad: When We recite it, follow its recitation;
versets.commentaire: “When We have gathered it in your heart and fixed it, then follow its recitation.” Qushayrī: the right posture is to follow, not to precede. First to receive in silence, then to recite. The whole spiritual life holds in this order: God gives, then man responds; the initiative belongs to grace, the fidelity to the servant.

versets.trad: then it is for Us, thereafter, to make it clear.
versets.commentaire: bayānahu (بَيانَهُ): its explanation. Qushayrī: “We shall make clear to you what it contains — the lawful, the unlawful, and the rest.” Not only does God give the text, He gives its meaning. True understanding is itself a gift: one does not force the sense of a divine word, one awaits it and receives it. To read is human, to understand is a grace.

versets.trad: No indeed! You love the fleeting present
versets.commentaire: al-ʿājila (العاجِلَة): the immediate, the hasty — this lower life. Qushayrī: “what drives them to deny the Resurrection is that they love the immediate, that is, abiding in this world.” The denial of the hereafter has its source not in the head but in the heart: one does not believe what one does not want. Attachment to the present shuts the eyes to the future.

mots.def: ʿājila: the present life, which hastens away.

versets.trad: and you neglect the life to come.
versets.commentaire: tadharūna (تَذَرُونَ): you leave, you forsake. Qushayrī: “you abandon the work for the hereafter” — or even: “you deny it.” To love the immediate and forsake the eternal: that is the fool's bargain denounced by the whole sūra. One prefers the little that passes to the all that abides, for want of believing the all exists.

versets.trad: That Day, there will be radiant faces
versets.commentaire: nāḍira (ناضِرَة): radiant, blooming, fresh with beauty. Qushayrī: “bright and beautiful — and it is so because it looks toward its Lord.” The radiance of the face is not a separate ornament: it is the reflection of the vision. One becomes luminous from what one contemplates. And Qushayrī notes: this radiance is a sign of expansion (basṭ), for these faces are in the state of full presence (ṣaḥw), without vertigo or stupor — “for subsistence in the encounter is more perfect than the encounter itself.”

versets.trad: turning their gaze toward their Lord.
versets.commentaire: The summit-verse of the whole sūra: ilā rabbihā nāẓira (إلى رَبِّها ناظِرَة) — “toward their Lord, gazing.” Qushayrī establishes that naẓar joined to ilā and ascribed to the face can mean nothing but real vision: God will create in their faces, in Paradise, the vision of Him, against the usual order of things. He finely adds: “the face does not see, but the eye in the face sees — as the river does not flow, but the water in the river flows.” And he adds the nuance of the realised: for the one who has been extinguished in the Existence (al-wujūd), the servant's annihilation in the Real is more complete still than the vision — for vision presupposes a seer who subsists.

mots.def: naẓara: to look; with ilā: to truly see.

versets.trad: That Day, there will be downcast faces
versets.commentaire: bāsira (باسِرَة): scowling, darkened, contracted, glosses Qushayrī. The contrast is total with the radiant faces: there, the blossoming of one who sees his Lord; here, the shrinking of one who sees himself deprived of Him. The face betrays the state of the heart: one carries on the brow the light or the night one has chosen.

mots.def: basara: to grow gloomy, to frown.

versets.trad: sensing that a crushing calamity awaits them.
versets.commentaire: fāqira (فاقِرَة): the calamity that breaks the spine. Qushayrī recalls the origin of the word: the branding with a hot iron on the camel's muzzle, reaching to the bone. The fāqira, here, is “abiding in the Fire” — the disaster that does not pass. And he notes: this foreboding itself is created by God in the faces, like a forerunning sign of the verdict.

mots.sens: that there be inflicted upon it

mots.def: fāqira: disaster that breaks the vertebrae.

versets.trad: No indeed! When the breath rises to the collarbones,
versets.commentaire: al-tarāqī (التَّراقي): the collarbones, the hollow of the throat. Qushayrī: “when their soul reaches the throat” — it is the instant of the death-agony, when the breath rises to the threshold of the body it is about to leave. The sūra descends here to the most concrete: no longer the distant Day, but the hour each will know, when the least detail of the visible tips into the invisible.

versets.trad: and it is asked: “Who can heal him?”,
versets.commentaire: man rāqin (مَنْ راقٍ): “who can heal by incantation?” Qushayrī gives two readings. The first, human: those around the dying man ask “is there a healer, a physician, a remedy?” — knowing there is none against death. The second, angelic and more striking: it is the angels around him who ask “who will bear his soul up — angels of mercy or angels of chastisement?” The soul, barely gone out, is already awaited, and its fate is decided at the instant of the threshold.

mots.def: qāla: to say; passive: it is said.
raqā: to heal by incantation, or to raise up.

versets.trad: and he realizes: “This is the parting!”,
versets.commentaire: al-firāq (الفِراق): the parting. Qushayrī: “the dying man then knows it is death.” The word ẓanna here no longer speaks of doubt but of the near-certainty that imposes itself. It is the hour when all illusions of duration fall away: one parts from the body, from kin, from possessions, from the world. For the prepared heart, this parting from everything is the eve of a union; for the other, a sheer tearing-away.

mots.def: ẓanna: to presume, be almost sure.

versets.trad: when leg twists against leg,
versets.commentaire: iltaffati l-sāqu bi-l-sāq (التَفَّتِ السَّاقُ بِالسَّاق): the dying man's legs coil one against the other in the final spasm. Qushayrī gives a deep reading: “thus the hardship of the end of the world joins the hardship of the beginning of the hereafter.” At the instant of the threshold, the two worlds touch: one leaves the one in pain, one enters the other in dread — or in peace, according to what one has prepared.

versets.trad: that Day, each is driven toward your Lord.
versets.commentaire: al-masāq (المَساق): the driving, the being led. Qushayrī: “the angels lead his soul toward God, where He commands them to bear it: either to ʿIlliyyīn — with varying degrees —, or to Sijjīn — with varying abysses.” And here is the unforgettable pearl: while men wash the body of the deceased, wrap it and pray over it, the Real clothes his soul in the adornments it deserves, washes it with the water of mercy, and prays over it with His angels. To the outer washing of the body answers the inner washing of the soul by grace.

mots.def: sāqa: to drive, lead; masāq: the being led.

versets.trad: He did not acknowledge the truth, nor did he pray;
versets.commentaire: A portrait of the denier. Qushayrī: “he neither believed in God nor prayed to Him; he denied and turned away from faith.” Two twin refusals: the refusal of the heart (not to acknowledge the truth) and the refusal of the body (not to prostrate). Faith that does not bend the knee is not yet faith, and prayer without inner acknowledgement is but a gesture; here both are lacking together.

versets.trad: on the contrary, he cried lies and turned away;
versets.commentaire: kadhdhaba wa-tawallā (كَذَّبَ وَتَوَلَّى): he denied and turned away. To the two omissions of the previous verse answer two positive acts of refusal: not only did he not believe, he actively treated the truth as a lie; not only did he not pray, he turned his back. Evil is not mere absence: it becomes movement, a repeated choice to draw away.

mots.def: kadhdhaba: to treat as a lie.

versets.trad: then he went off to his people, strutting.
versets.commentaire: yatamaṭṭā (يَتَمَطَّى): he walks puffed up, glosses Qushayrī: “he struts and swaggers.” The summit of the portrait: not content to refuse, here he returns to his people swollen with himself, as if he had triumphed. Pride is the natural outcome of denial: having rejected what is greater than oneself, one fancies oneself the greatest. The gait betrays the heart.

mots.def: tamaṭṭā: to swagger, walk with arrogance.

versets.trad: Woe to you, and woe again!
versets.commentaire: awlā laka (أَوْلى لَكَ): an expression of threat. Qushayrī: the Arabs, to call down misfortune upon someone, would say “awlā laka!”; the repetition increases its force. He offers the gloss: “woe to you the day you live, woe to you the day you die, woe to you the day you are raised, woe to you the day you enter the Fire.” The threat encircles man on all sides, present and to come.

mots.def: awlā laka: a formula of threat, “this is your due.”

versets.trad: Again, woe to you, and woe again!
versets.commentaire: The threat is redoubled, hammered home. Qushayrī recalls that tradition reports the Prophet may have addressed these verses to Abū Jahl, the harshest adversary of the early days. The repetition is not merely rhetorical: it imitates the ineluctable, what comes back and comes back again. To the pride that thought itself safe answers this stubborn reminder: you will not escape.

mots.sens: woe to you, and woe again

versets.trad: Does man think he will be left to himself, unbound?
versets.commentaire: sudan (سُدى): left to abandon, with no charge and no account to render. Qushayrī: “neglected, with no obligation? It is not so.” A decisive question: does man imagine himself created without purpose, free of all responsibility? But a creature endowed with hearing, sight, and heart is not a whim without sequel. To be called to account is no threat: it is the very dignity of a being who matters in the eyes of his Creator.

versets.ar: أَلَمْ يَكُ نُطْفَةً مِّن مَّنِيٍّ يُمْنَىٰ
versets.tr: a-lam yaku nuṭfatan min maniyyin yumnā
versets.trad: Was he not a drop of fluid emitted?
versets.commentaire: nuṭfa (نُطْفَة): a drop. Qushayrī: “a drop placed in the womb.” The argument for resurrection closes upon the origin: remember whence you came. This tiny drop, without form or name, became this being who doubts and struts. He who drew so much from so little can well draw life out of death. The proud man of verse 33 is sent back to his liquid source.

versets.trad: Then it became a clinging clot which God formed and fashioned.
versets.commentaire: ʿalaqa (عَلَقَة): a clot, clinging blood; sawwā (سَوَّى): He fashioned, set in just proportion. Qushayrī: “He arranged its limbs in its mother's womb, ordered its parts according to its form.” From drop to clot, from clot to form: a patient ascent in which each stage heralds the next. The same verb sawwā that speaks of the fashioning of the heaven (s. 79, v. 28) here speaks of the fashioning of the embryo — the same art, the same Artisan.

versets.trad: From it He made a pair: the male and the female.
versets.commentaire: al-zawjayn (الزَّوْجَيْن): the two members of the pair. Qushayrī: “from the same drop: if He wills, the male; if He wills, the female; if He wills, both.” From a single, undifferentiated origin God draws the polarity that makes life. The same fluid, and yet the masculine and the feminine: a sign that the richness of the created lies not in the diversity of origins but in the freedom of the Creator, who distinguishes what He wills from what He wills.

mots.racine: ذ ك ر / أ ن ث

versets.ar: أَلَيْسَ ذَٰلِكَ بِقَادِرٍ عَلَىٰ أَن يُحْيِيَ الْمَوْتَىٰ
versets.tr: a-laysa dhālika bi-qādirin ʿalā an yuḥyiya l-mawtā
versets.trad: Is the One who did this not able to bring the dead to life?
versets.commentaire: A closing verse, in the form of a question whose answer lies within it. Qushayrī: “The One who had power over all this — has He not the power to give life to the dead? — an interrogation with the meaning of affirmation.” The whole sūra returns to its starting point (v. 3): the doubt about resurrection collapses before the evidence of the first creation. To remake is easier than to make; and He who could draw a living being from a drop will be at no loss to draw it from the dust. The circle is closed: the end answers the beginning.
